owasp_top10: A08:2021 - Software and Data Integrity Failures
cwe: CWE-732 - Incorrect Permission Assignment for Critical Resource

threat.title: Docker Container Escape via runC Vulnerabilities and Privileged Containers
threat.description: Container escape vulnerabilities allow attackers to break out of container isolation and gain root access to the host system. This is achieved through exploiting runC vulnerabilities, misconfigured privileged containers, or unsafe mount configurations. Once escaped, attackers can compromise the entire host, access other containers, steal secrets, and establish persistent backdoors.

attack.scenario:
1. Attacker gains ability to deploy malicious container image or exploits existing vulnerable container
2. Deploys container with privileged flag (--privileged) or dangerous capabilities (CAP_SYS_ADMIN, CAP_SYS_PTRACE)
3. Exploits runC vulnerability (CVE-2019-5736, CVE-2024-21626, or CVE-2025-31133) to overwrite host runC binary
4. Triggers container restart or new container spawn, executing malicious runC code on host
5. Gains root access to host filesystem via /proc/self/exe or masked path manipulation
6. Establishes persistence, exfiltrates secrets from other containers, pivots to cloud infrastructure
prerequisites: Ability to deploy containers (compromised CI/CD pipeline, Kubernetes RBAC misconfiguration, or Docker API exposure)
Vulnerable runC version (< 1.1.12) or misconfigured container runtime
Privileged container mode OR dangerous Linux capabilities enabled
Host filesystem accessible via bind mounts or procfs manipulation

cve_references.description: runC container escape via /proc/self/exe overwrite allowing host runC binary manipulation
runC file descriptor leak allowing container escape via leaked file descriptors to host filesystem
runC masked path abuse via symlink replacement of /dev/null to procfs files (core_pattern, sysrq-trigger)

real_world_breaches.impact: Unauthorized cryptocurrency mining in Tesla's AWS infrastructure, exposure of proprietary telemetry data stored in S3
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Kubernetes console exposed without authentication, allowing container deployment and cluster admin access

real_world_breaches.incident: Leaky Vessels Global Container Escape Campaign
real_world_breaches.impact: 80% of cloud environments vulnerable to CVE-2024-21626, affecting Docker, Kubernetes, and major cloud providers
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Widespread use of vulnerable runC versions across container platforms

bug_bounty_reports.summary: Container escape via privileged container with host path mount allowing complete host compromise

security_research.title: NIST SP 800-190: Application Container Security Guide
security_research.key_finding: Container escape is one of five major container security threats, requiring defense-in-depth approach

security_research.title: runC Container Breakout Vulnerabilities: A Technical Overview
security_research.key_finding: Three critical runC vulnerabilities (CVE-2025-31133, CVE-2025-52565, CVE-2025-52881) bypass LSM protections

mitigations.title: Implement Docker Security Profiles (AppArmor/SELinux)
mitigations.description: Apply mandatory access control (MAC) security profiles to restrict container capabilities and prevent host filesystem access. AppArmor and SELinux provide kernel-level enforcement that blocks container escape attempts even when vulnerabilities exist.

code_example.code:
#!/bin/bash
# Create custom AppArmor profile for containers
sudo cat > /etc/apparmor.d/docker-secure << 'EOF'
#include <tunables/global>

profile docker-secure flags=(attach_disconnected,mediate_deleted) {
  #include <abstractions/base>
  
  # Deny access to host filesystem roots
  deny / r,
  deny /proc/sys/** w,
  deny /proc/sysrq-trigger rwklx,
  deny /proc/sys/kernel/core_pattern rwklx,
  deny /sys/** w,
  
  # Allow container-specific paths
  /var/lib/docker/** rw,
  /dev/null rw,
  /dev/zero rw,
  /dev/random r,
  /dev/urandom r,
  
  # Deny dangerous capabilities
  deny capability sys_admin,
  deny capability sys_ptrace,
  deny capability sys_module,
  deny capability dac_override,
  
  # Block runC binary access
  deny /usr/bin/runc rwklx,
  deny /usr/bin/docker-runc rwklx,
  deny /proc/*/exe rwklx,
}
EOF

# Load the profile
sudo apparmor_parser -r /etc/apparmor.d/docker-secure

# Run container with AppArmor profile
docker run -d \
  --security-opt apparmor=docker-secure \
  --name secure-app \
  nginx:latest

# For SELinux (alternative to AppArmor)
# Enable SELinux enforcing mode
sudo setenforce 1

# Run container with SELinux label
docker run -d \
  --security-opt label=type:svirt_sandbox_file_t \
  --name secure-app-selinux \
  nginx:latest

# Verify security profile is applied
docker inspect secure-app | grep -A 5 SecurityOpt

nist_csf: PR.PT-3 - Access to systems and assets is controlled
validation_steps: Verify AppArmor/SELinux is enabled: aa-status or sestatus
Test profile blocks /proc/sys writes: docker exec <container> sh -c 'echo 1 > /proc/sys/kernel/core_pattern' (should fail)
Confirm profile loaded: docker inspect <container> | grep SecurityOpt
Test container cannot access host filesystem: docker exec <container> ls /host (should fail)

mitigations.title: Deploy Rootless Docker with User Namespace Remapping
mitigations.description: Run Docker daemon and containers as non-root user using user namespace remapping. This ensures that even if container escape occurs, the attacker only gains unprivileged user access on the host, not root privileges.

code_example.code:
#!/bin/bash
# Install rootless Docker (one-time setup)
curl -fsSL https://get.docker.com/rootless | sh

# Configure system prerequisites
sudo sh -c 'echo "$(whoami):100000:65536" >> /etc/subuid'
sudo sh -c 'echo "$(whoami):100000:65536" >> /etc/subgid'

# Enable systemd user service
systemctl --user enable docker.service
systemctl --user start docker.service

# Set Docker socket environment variable
export DOCKER_HOST=unix://$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/docker.sock

# Add to shell profile for persistence
echo 'export DOCKER_HOST=unix://$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/docker.sock' >> ~/.bashrc
echo 'export PATH=/home/$(whoami)/bin:$PATH' >> ~/.bashrc

# Verify rootless mode
docker info | grep -i rootless
# Should show: "rootless: true"

# Run container with user namespace remapping (alternative approach)
# If rootless mode not available, use userns-remap
sudo cat > /etc/docker/daemon.json << 'EOF'
{
  "userns-remap": "default",
  "default-ulimits": {
    "nofile": {
      "Name": "nofile",
      "Hard": 64000,
      "Soft": 64000
    }
  }
}
EOF

# Restart Docker daemon
sudo systemctl restart docker

# Verify user namespace is active
docker run --rm alpine cat /proc/self/uid_map
# Should show remapped UIDs: 0 100000 65536

# Test: Container root is unprivileged on host
docker run -d --name test nginx
ps aux | grep nginx
# nginx processes should run as remapped user (100000+), not root

iso27001_controls: A.9.2.3 - Management of privileged access rights
validation_steps: Check rootless mode: docker info | grep -i rootless
Verify user namespace mapping: cat /proc/$(docker inspect -f '{{.State.Pid}}' <container>)/uid_map
Test privilege escalation blocked: docker exec <container> su - (should fail)
Confirm host processes run as non-root: ps aux | grep docker

mitigations.title: Drop Dangerous Linux Capabilities and Disable Privileged Mode
mitigations.description: Remove unnecessary Linux capabilities from containers and explicitly forbid --privileged flag. Capabilities like CAP_SYS_ADMIN, CAP_SYS_PTRACE, and CAP_SYS_MODULE enable container escape vectors and must be dropped.

code_example.code:
# Kubernetes Pod Security Standards (Restricted Profile)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: secure-app
  labels:
    app: secure-app
spec:
  securityContext:
    runAsNonRoot: true
    runAsUser: 1000
    fsGroup: 1000
    seccompProfile:
      type: RuntimeDefault
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:1.25-alpine
    securityContext:
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
      readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
      privileged: false
      capabilities:
        drop:
          - ALL  # Drop all capabilities
        add:
          - NET_BIND_SERVICE  # Only add what's needed
    volumeMounts:
    - name: tmp
      mountPath: /tmp
    - name: cache
      mountPath: /var/cache/nginx
  volumes:
  - name: tmp
    emptyDir: {}
  - name: cache
    emptyDir: {}

---
# Docker Compose with capability dropping
version: '3.8'
services:
  app:
    image: nginx:1.25-alpine
    cap_drop:
      - ALL
    cap_add:
      - NET_BIND_SERVICE
    security_opt:
      - no-new-privileges:true
    read_only: true
    user: "1000:1000"
    tmpfs:
      - /tmp
      - /var/cache/nginx

---
# Docker CLI with hardened security
#!/bin/bash
docker run -d \
  --name secure-nginx \
  --cap-drop ALL \
  --cap-add NET_BIND_SERVICE \
  --security-opt no-new-privileges:true \
  --read-only \
  --tmpfs /tmp \
  --tmpfs /var/cache/nginx \
  --user 1000:1000 \
  --pids-limit 100 \
  --memory 512m \
  --cpus 0.5 \
  nginx:1.25-alpine

# Kubernetes admission controller to block privileged containers
apiVersion: v1
kind: PodSecurityPolicy
metadata:
  name: restricted
spec:
  privileged: false  # Block --privileged flag
  allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
  requiredDropCapabilities:
    - ALL
  allowedCapabilities:
    - NET_BIND_SERVICE
  volumes:
    - 'configMap'
    - 'emptyDir'
    - 'projected'
    - 'secret'
    - 'downwardAPI'
    - 'persistentVolumeClaim'
  hostNetwork: false
  hostIPC: false
  hostPID: false
  runAsUser:
    rule: 'MustRunAsNonRoot'
  seLinux:
    rule: 'RunAsAny'
  fsGroup:
    rule: 'RunAsAny'
  readOnlyRootFilesystem: true

validation_steps: Audit existing containers: docker ps --format '{{.Names}}' | xargs -I {} docker inspect {} | grep -i privileged
Test capability drop: docker exec <container> capsh --print (should show minimal caps)
Verify no new privileges: docker inspect <container> | grep NoNewPrivileges (should be true)
Block privileged container test: docker run --privileged alpine (should be blocked by admission controller)

indicators: Container processes accessing /proc/self/exe or host runC binary
Container mounting host filesystem paths like /dev, /sys, /proc
Privileged container deployments or containers with CAP_SYS_ADMIN
Container processes running as UID 0 (root) on host
Unusual container restarts or runC process crashes

log_queries.query:
index=container sourcetype=docker:daemon OR sourcetype=linux_secure
| search ("privileged" OR "CAP_SYS_ADMIN" OR "CAP_SYS_PTRACE" OR "/proc/self/exe" OR "core_pattern" OR "sysrq-trigger")
| eval severity=case(
    match(_raw, "privileged.*true"), "critical",
    match(_raw, "CAP_SYS_ADMIN|CAP_SYS_PTRACE"), "high",
    match(_raw, "proc/self/exe|core_pattern|sysrq-trigger"), "critical",
    1=1, "medium"
  )
| stats count by host, container_name, severity, _time
| where severity IN ("critical", "high")
| sort -_time

event.dataset: (docker.container OR linux.auditd) AND (
  container.security_context.privileged: true OR
  process.args: (*CAP_SYS_ADMIN* OR *CAP_SYS_PTRACE* OR *CAP_SYS_MODULE*) OR
  file.path: (/proc/self/exe OR /proc/sys/kernel/core_pattern OR /proc/sysrq-trigger) OR
  process.name: runc AND event.action: (exec OR write)
)

tools.name: Falco
tools.usage: Detects container escape behaviors using eBPF kernel instrumentation and predefined rules for runC exploitation

tools.name: Tracee
tools.usage: Uses eBPF to detect container escape attempts, including procfs manipulation and capability abuse

tools.name: Aqua Runtime Protection
tools.usage: Real-time detection of container escapes with behavioral analysis and anomaly detection

tools.name: Sysdig Secure
tools.usage: Monitors container runtime for escape indicators and CVE exploitation attempts
